toctou_file_path.assumption: File at path X is the same file that was loaded/executed

trigger_patterns.context: Driver or service resolves a process image path then reads/hashes the file at that path

bypass_techniques: NTFS hardlink swap between process load and driver check
Symlink redirect to trusted binary
Directory junction pointing to attacker-controlled location
Rename race on the executable path
toctou_file_path.real_world: AsIO3.sys CVE-2025-3464 — hardlink swap bypasses SHA256 ImageHashCheck
toctou_file_path.question: Does this check read the file on disk, or verify the loaded binary in memory? Can the file at the path change between check and use?

toctou_shared_memory.assumption: Value in shared memory stays the same between validation and use

trigger_patterns.context: Kernel reads a value from user-writable memory, validates it, then reads it again

bypass_techniques: Racing thread flipping the value between valid and malicious
userfaultfd to stall kernel between reads
FUSE filesystem returning different data per read
mmap + mprotect race
toctou_shared_memory.real_world: Hyper-V vmswitch double-fetch — section_idx validated then re-read from guest ring buffer
toctou_shared_memory.question: Is this value read once into kernel memory and reused, or re-read from the shared location?

toctou_multifetch_alloc_windows.assumption: The 16/32/64-bit field at *(buf + N) yields the same value on every read; alloc size and copy length read it independently and presume agreement.
toctou_multifetch_alloc_windows.attack_class: Windows-kernel multi-fetch into ExAllocatePool* (CVE-2026-3006 / WinFSP class)

trigger_patterns.context: Same `*(<type>*)(<var>+<off>)` deref appears as ExAllocatePool* size argument AND elsewhere in the same function as bound check or copy length, on ≥2 distinct lines.

racing_source_classes.irp_user_buffer: METHOD_NEITHER IOCTL — user owns the buffer, race trivial. Static-detect via Irp->UserBuffer / Type3InputBuffer / SystemBuffer in the assignment RHS.
racing_source_classes.mdl_mapped_user_buffer: METHOD_IN_DIRECT / METHOD_OUT_DIRECT — MmGetSystemAddressForMdlSafe / MmMapLockedPagesSpecifyCache returns a kernel-VA pointer into user-locked pages; the user mapping is still alive in the calling process. User-mutable unless the function deep-copies via ProbeForRead+RtlCopyMemory or caller-snapshots into a kernel buffer.
racing_source_classes.ccmap_file_cache_page: Bytes from CcMapData/CcPinRead — coherent ONLY if file is opened FILE_SHARE_WRITE; pre-flight test required.
racing_source_classes.guest_shared_ring: Hyper-V VMBus / virtio ring header — guest-mutable.
racing_source_classes.kernel_resident: this/global/DAT_*/parameter from ExAllocate output — not user-mutable, FALSE_POSITIVE.

recognised_defenses.kernel_field_snapshot: Caller copies user fields onto kernel stack and forwards `&local_a`. Inner multi-fetch reads kernel stack — race-immune.
recognised_defenses.file_share_exclusivity: Kernel driver opens the source file with FILE_SHARE_NONE. Second user process can't acquire writable handle; CcMapData coherence is moot.
recognised_defenses.probeforread_plus_rtlcopy: ProbeForRead + RtlCopyMemory into kernel-pool deep-copies the user struct before downstream reads.

bypass_techniques: Pin attacker thread to a different CPU than the syscall thread
Tight loop alternating between valid and malicious value at the suspect offset
For Cc-mapped sources: open the file with FILE_SHARE_WRITE before the kernel driver opens it (rarely possible)
For IRP user buffer: standard SMP race via second-thread WriteFile/memory write
toctou_multifetch_alloc_windows.question: For each suspect *(var+off) deref: (1) what racing-source class? (2) does the caller perform kernel_field_snapshot? (3) for ccmap source, does FILE_SHARE_NONE close the race?

toctou_handle_reuse.assumption: Handle refers to the same object between check and use

trigger_patterns.context: Code validates a handle's type or permissions, then uses it after a potential close/reopen window

bypass_techniques: Close handle in racing thread, force handle number reuse with different object type
Exploit handle table recycling to get same handle value pointing to different object
toctou_handle_reuse.question: Can the handle be closed and recycled between the type check and the use?

process_name_identity.assumption: Process executable name or path substring equals process identity

trigger_patterns.context: Driver or service checks if calling process name contains a trusted substring

bypass_techniques: Name attacker executable to contain the trusted substring
Place executable in directory whose path contains the substring
Create hardlink with matching name to attacker binary
process_name_identity.real_world: AsIO3.sys also does substring match for 'AsusCertService' in path — any directory with that name works
process_name_identity.question: Is this checking the full verified identity of the caller, or just a substring of a spoofable path?

size_checked_vs_used.assumption: Size validated once remains valid when used in allocation/copy
size_checked_vs_used.attack_class: Integer overflow / truncation between check and use

trigger_patterns.context: Size is validated against a maximum, then used in arithmetic (multiply, add) before allocation

bypass_techniques: Integer overflow in size * element_count wraps to small allocation
Truncation from 64-bit to 32-bit size between check and alloc
Sign confusion: negative value passes < MAX check, becomes huge unsigned
size_checked_vs_used.question: Is the EXACT validated value used in allocation, or does it go through arithmetic that could overflow?

pointer_validity.assumption: Pointer validated at check time is still valid at use time

trigger_patterns.context: Object freed on one path, pointer still used on another path or by another thread

bypass_techniques: Trigger free path, then reclaim the freed memory with controlled data
Race condition between free and use across threads
Reference count manipulation to trigger early free
pointer_validity.question: Can this object be freed while a reference to it still exists? Is the refcount correctly managed?

kernel_api_as_safe_wrapper.assumption: Calling a kernel API with user-controlled args is safe because it's a kernel function

trigger_patterns.context: IOCTL handler passes user-controlled value directly to a kernel API

bypass_techniques: ObfDereferenceObject on arbitrary address = decrement-by-one at (addr - 0x30)
ExFreePool on controlled address = arbitrary free for UAF
ZwClose on arbitrary handle value = close handles in other processes
kernel_api_as_safe_wrapper.real_world: AsIO3.sys IOCTL 0xA0402450 — ObfDereferenceObject with user-supplied address = arbitrary kernel memory decrement
kernel_api_as_safe_wrapper.question: Does this IOCTL pass user-controlled values directly to kernel APIs? What side-effects do those APIs have beyond their intended purpose?

single_check_stays_valid.assumption: A condition checked once remains true for the duration of the operation

trigger_patterns.context: Security check performed without holding a lock that protects the checked state

bypass_techniques: Race thread mutates the checked state between check and use
Signal handler interrupts between check and use
Callback invoked between check and use that changes state
single_check_stays_valid.question: Is the checked state protected by a lock during both check and use? Can another thread/interrupt modify it?

access_control_completeness.assumption: All paths to sensitive functionality go through the access check

trigger_patterns.context: Driver validates caller on IRP_MJ_DEVICE_CONTROL but not on other IRP types

bypass_techniques: Use IRP_MJ_INTERNAL_DEVICE_CONTROL which skips validation
Use IRP_MJ_READ/WRITE directly if they expose the same functionality
Direct IOCTL from kernel mode bypasses userspace validation
access_control_completeness.question: Is this access check applied on ALL paths to the sensitive operation, or only on the obvious one?

junction_on_write.assumption: Path resolved by privileged code refers to a normal directory under the user profile

trigger_patterns.context: SYSTEM service writes/copies/deletes under a path the low-priv user can replace with a junction

bypass_techniques: mklink /J victim_dir target_privileged_dir
Use NtCreateFile with FILE_OPEN_REPARSE_POINT to set mount point
Combine with oplock to swap mid-operation
junction_on_write.real_world: BlueHammer CVE-2026-33825, OEM uninstaller LPE family
junction_on_write.question: Does the privileged side resolve junctions before the operation, or does it write through them?

oplock_on_check.assumption: File contents and identity stay constant from check to act

trigger_patterns.context: Privileged side performs verify/hash followed by another open/read of the same path

bypass_techniques: FSCTL_REQUEST_OPLOCK on the target file as the user
On oplock break, swap target via hardlink retarget or rename
oplock_on_check.question: Does the operation re-open or re-read the file after an integrity check?

rpc_caller_trust.assumption: RPC peer identity from PID/path/binding is trustworthy

trigger_patterns.context: RPC handler retrieves caller info via PID or path string and uses it for authorization

bypass_techniques: PID reuse race
Hardlink to trusted-named binary then exec to spoof image path identity
Parent-PID spoof to fake trusted ancestor
rpc_caller_trust.question: Is caller identity established by token integrity / SID, or by an attacker-influenceable name?

com_autoelevate.assumption: CLSID with Elevation\Enabled=1 should only be invoked by genuinely-elevated callers
com_autoelevate.attack_class: COM autoElevate via CoCreateInstanceAsAdmin coercion (Shape C)

trigger_patterns.context: Elevated COM object exposing a privileged primitive (FileWrite/RegistryWrite/ShellExec)

bypass_techniques: Mock-folder %SystemRoot% redirection
DLL planting in RunDll32 search path
Registry CLSID hijack via per-user TreatAs
com_autoelevate.real_world: Numerous fileless UAC bypasses, FodHelper, ComputerDefaults, Forshaw Admin Protection bypass series
com_autoelevate.question: Does the COM server validate caller integrity, or trust the elevation moniker?

vss_previous_version.assumption: Shadow copies are read-only historical snapshots

trigger_patterns.context: Privileged worker reads or writes through a path containing a shadow-copy device name

bypass_techniques: Create attacker-controlled shadow copy mounted as a subpath of a SYSTEM-touched directory
Combine with junction to make SYSTEM operate in the shadow namespace
vss_previous_version.question: Does the path resolution code reject \\.\GLOBALROOT or shadow-copy device names?

cloud_files_placeholder.assumption: Reads from a path return real on-disk bytes

trigger_patterns.context: Privileged code reads a file under a Cloud Files sync root

bypass_techniques: Register attacker-owned sync provider over a directory the privileged worker reads
Provider returns different content per fetch — TOCTOU between read1 and read2
cloud_files_placeholder.question: Does the privileged worker handle FILE_ATTRIBUTE_RECALL_ON_DATA_ACCESS, or treat placeholder files as regular files?

update_worker_staging.assumption: User-writable staging dir contains only the worker's own artifacts
update_worker_staging.attack_class: Update / repair / rollback worker abuse (Shape D)

trigger_patterns.context: SYSTEM worker scans %ProgramData% / %TEMP% / Installer cache and acts on found files

bypass_techniques: Pre-stage attacker DLL where worker calls LoadLibrary with non-fully-qualified name
Replace expected file with junction to privileged location
MSI rollback log contains attacker-injected restore entries
update_worker_staging.real_world: CVE-2025-21204 (WU stack), BlueHammer (Defender), Mandiant third-party installer LPE catalog
update_worker_staging.question: Does the worker fully-qualify all paths and verify file ownership before acting?

se_impersonate_coercion.assumption: Service-side accept of unauthenticated callbacks preserves caller integrity

trigger_patterns.context: Process holds SeImpersonatePrivilege, accepts an inbound auth and impersonates

bypass_techniques: Coerce SYSTEM service to authenticate to attacker-controlled named pipe / RPC endpoint
Capture the auth, impersonate to reach SYSTEM token
se_impersonate_coercion.real_world: PrintSpoofer, RoguePotato, GodPotato, EfsPotato (any process with SeImpersonate)
se_impersonate_coercion.question: Can a low-priv process force the target service to authenticate outbound to me?
